content: Immunotherapy harnesses the immune system to fight cancer. Checkpoint inhibitors block proteins that prevent T-cells from attacking tumors. PD-1 (programmed death-1) receptors on T-cells bind PD-L1 on tumor cells, suppressing immune response; anti-PD-1 antibodies (pembrolizumab, nivolumab) or anti-PD-L1 (atezolizumab, durvalumab) restore T-cell activity. CTLA-4 (cytotoxic T-lymphocyte antigen-4) regulates early T-cell activation; ipilimumab blocks CTLA-4. Other targets: LAG-3 (relatlimab), TIGIT (investigational). CAR-T cell therapy engineers patient T-cells to express chimeric antigen receptors targeting CD19 (lymphoma, leukemia) or BCMA (myeloma). Bispecific antibodies (blinatumomab) engage T-cells directly with tumor cells. Cancer vaccines (sipuleucel-T for prostate) and oncolytic viruses (T-VEC for melanoma) are emerging approaches.
